## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — ROI, Market Share & Growth Rate Calculation

### 🎯 Problem Statement
A start-up packaged millet-based snack company reports the following for FY 2023–24:
- **Total Investment** = ₹50,00,000
- **Net Profit (after tax)** = ₹8,00,000
- **Sales** = ₹80,00,000
- **Total Indian millet-snack market sales** = ₹400,00,00,000
- **Sales 2 years ago (FY 2021–22)** = ₹40,00,000

**Required:**
1. Calculate the ROI.
2. Calculate the market share.
3. Calculate the CAGR over 2 years.
4. If the company spent ₹12,00,000 on marketing and acquired 6,000 new customers, calculate CAC.
5. If the average order value is ₹400 and customers order 5 times a year for 3 years, calculate LTV and CAC:LTV ratio.

### 📐 Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part 1: Return on Investment

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 16\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The company generates **₹16 of profit for every ₹100 invested** — a respectable return for a start-up.

#### Part 2: Market Share

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Market Share} &= \frac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{400{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 0.20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite ₹80 lakh in sales, the company commands only **0.2% of the market** — indicating enormous growth potential and a long road to scale.

#### Part 3: Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAGR} &= \left( \frac{\text{End Value}}{\text{Start Value}} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1 \\[6pt]
&= \left( \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}} - 1 \\[6pt]
&= (2)^{0.5} - 1 \\[6pt]
&= 1.4142 - 1 \\[6pt]
&= 0.4142 \text{ or } 41.42\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The company has grown at an impressive **41.42% CAGR** over 2 years — typical of high-growth food-tech start-ups.

#### Part 4: Customer Acquisition Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAC} &= \frac{\text{Total Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers Acquired}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{6{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= ₹200 \text{ per customer}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The company spends **₹200 to acquire each new customer** — within reasonable bounds for D2C food brands.

#### Part 5: Customer Lifetime Value and Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LTV} &= \text{Average Order Value} \times \text{Frequency} \times \text{Retention Period} \\[6pt]
&= 400 \times 5 \times 3 \\[6pt]
&= ₹6{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{CAC:LTV Ratio} &= \frac{200}{6{,}000} = \frac{1}{30} = 0.033
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The company spends **₹1 to earn ₹30 in lifetime value** — exceptionally healthy unit economics, ideal for scaling.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Working Capital Management

### 🎯 Problem Statement
A small dairy in Wayanad reports the following balance sheet items:
- **Current Assets** = ₹15,00,000 (Inventory: ₹8,00,000; Cash: ₹3,00,000; Debtors: ₹4,00,000)
- **Current Liabilities** = ₹8,00,000

**Required:**
1. Calculate Working Capital.
2. Calculate Current Ratio and Quick Ratio.
3. Comment on liquidity.

### 📐 Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part 1: Working Capital

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Working Capital} &= \text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities} \\[6pt]
&= 15{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 \\[6pt]
&= ₹7{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The dairy has a positive working capital of ₹7 lakh — sufficient to fund day-to-day operations.

#### Part 2: Current Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= 1.875
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The dairy can cover each ₹1 of short-term obligations with ₹1.875 of short-term assets — **healthy** (ideal range: 1.5 to 2.0).

#### Part 3: Quick Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quick Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Current Assets} - \text{Inventory}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= 0.875
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A quick ratio below 1 indicates that the dairy's **liquidity depends heavily on inventory liquidation** (i.e., selling milk products quickly). This is risky for a perishable goods business — the entrepreneur should improve cash reserves or reduce inventory holding time.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation — Safety Stock Formula (Justification)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU may ask: *"Why is safety stock calculated as the difference between maximum and average lead time demand?"*

### Derivation
1. **Lead Time Demand** = Consumption Rate × Lead Time
2. **Average Lead Time Demand** = Avg Consumption × Avg Lead Time
3. **Maximum Lead Time Demand** = Max Consumption × Max Lead Time
4. **Safety Stock** exists to buffer against:
   - Higher-than-average consumption (demand variability)
   - Longer-than-average lead time (supply variability)
5. Hence, the safest buffer is the **worst-case minus the average-case** consumption during lead time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Safety Stock} &= \text{Max Lead Time Demand} - \text{Average Lead Time Demand} \\[6pt]
\text{Reorder Level} &= \text{Safety Stock} + \text{Average Lead Time Demand} \\[6pt]
&= \text{Max Lead Time Demand}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Reorder when worst could happen, not when average does."*

# 📘 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

> **"Differentiate between FSSAI License and Trade License. Why are both mandatory for a food business in India?"**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
- **FSSAI License** is a regulatory permit issued by the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, certifying that the food business complies with prescribed food safety and hygiene standards.
- **Trade License** is a permit issued by the local municipal authority (Corporation, Municipality, or Panchayat) authorizing the business to carry on a particular trade at a specified location, ensuring compliance with local zoning, public safety, and building regulations.

**Key Differences (1.5 Marks):**
| Parameter | FSSAI License | Trade License |
|---|---|---|
| Issuing Authority | FSSAI (Central/State) | Local Municipal Body |
| Focus | Food safety & standards | Location & commercial activity |
| Governing Act | FSS Act, 2006 | Municipal/Local Body Act |
| Mandatory For | All food businesses | All commercial establishments |

**Why Both Mandatory (0.5 Mark):**
A food business must have both because FSSAI ensures the **food product** is safe, while the Trade License ensures the **business premises** is legally authorized to operate. Operating without either is a punishable offense.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

> **"List and briefly explain any three challenges faced by food start-ups in India."**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

**1. Financial Challenge (1 Mark):**
High initial capital expenditure for equipment (autoclaves, cold storage), seasonal working capital crunch, and high burn rate. Mitigation: bootstrapping, MSME loans, angel investment.

**2. Regulatory Challenge (1 Mark):**
Complex multi-license compliance (FSSAI, Trade License, GST, Factory License). Frequent amendments to food safety norms increase compliance burden. Mitigation: hire compliance officer, use FSSAI online portal.

**3. Operational Challenge (1 Mark):**
Supply chain volatility (raw material price fluctuations), cold chain breakdowns causing spoilage, and quality inconsistency across batches. Mitigation: multi-vendor sourcing, IoT monitoring, statistical process control.

> [!NOTE]

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

> **"(a) Discuss the six functional pillars of effective management of a small food business. How do they collectively ensure business sustainability? (7 Marks)"**
>
> **"(b) A small-scale spice processing unit in Kerala has the following data:**
> - **Fixed Cost per month: ₹3,00,000**
> - **Variable Cost per kg: ₹120**
> - **Selling Price per kg: ₹200**
> - **Monthly Capacity: 5,000 kg**
>
> **Calculate:**
> - **(i) Break-Even Point in units and in sales value (3 Marks)**
> - **(ii) Contribution Margin Ratio (1 Mark)**
> - **(iii) Sales units required to earn a monthly profit of ₹2,00,000 (2 Marks)**
> - **(iv) Comment on the operational feasibility of the business (1 Mark)"**

### ✅ Model Answer

#### Part (a): Six Functional Pillars (7 Marks)

**1. Strategic Planning (1.2 Marks):**
Defining the long-term direction of the business using tools like SWOT, PESTLE, and Porter's Five Forces. Example: A spice unit deciding to focus on export markets by leveraging Kerala's GI-tagged pepper.

**2. Financial Management (1.2 Marks):**
Managing working capital, cash flow, break-even, and ROI. Small food businesses must maintain 3–6 months of operating cash reserve and monitor financial ratios (Current Ratio, Quick Ratio) monthly.

**3. Human Resource Management (1.2 Marks):**
Recruiting skilled food technicians, training staff on GMP/GHP/HACCP, and retaining talent through performance-based incentives. Critical in a high-turnover industry.

**4. Production & Operations Management (1.2 Marks):**
Production planning based on seasonal demand, FIFO inventory management, and quality control through sensory and microbiological testing.

**5. Marketing Management (1.1 Marks):**
Applying the 4Ps framework, leveraging digital marketing (social media, SEO, influencer collaborations), and using packaging as a silent salesman.

**6. Regulatory & Compliance Management (1.1 Marks):**
FSSAI labelling compliance, Legal Metrology Act adherence, environmental clearances, and maintaining audit-ready documentation.

**Collective Sustainability (Bonus 0.5 Mark, integrated within):**
All six pillars are interdependent — weak financial control undermines HR retention; poor quality control damages marketing; non-compliance invites penalties that erode profits. Their integrated application ensures the business survives the start-up valley of death and attains scale.

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Fixed Cost (FC) = ₹3,00,000
- Variable Cost (VC) per kg = ₹120
- Selling Price (SP) per kg = ₹200
- Monthly Capacity = 5,000 kg

**Step 1 — Contribution per kg (Working Step, 0.5 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contribution per kg} &= \text{SP} - \text{VC} = 200 - 120 = ₹80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Break-Even Point in Units (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP (units)} &= \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Contribution per Unit}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{80} \\[6pt]
&= 3{,}750 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Break-Even Point in Sales Value (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP (₹)} &= 3{,}750 \times 200 = ₹7{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Contribution Margin Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CM Ratio} &= \frac{80}{200} \times 100 = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Units for Target Profit of ₹2,00,000 (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Required Units} &= \frac{\text{FC} + \text{Target Profit}}{\text{Contribution}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000}{80} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{80} \\[6pt]
&= 6{,}250 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Operational Feasibility Comment (0.5 Mark):**
Required volume (6,250 kg) **exceeds** monthly capacity (5,000 kg) by 1,250 kg. **The business cannot achieve the target profit at current capacity.** The entrepreneur must either increase capacity, raise selling price, or reduce variable cost. This is **operationally infeasible as currently configured**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Numerical Section:**
> 1. **Do NOT** compute BEP using sales value formula with wrong denominator — students often write $\frac{\text{FC}}{\text{CM Ratio}}$ and forget the multiplication.
> 2. **Do NOT** report BEP in units without showing the contribution calculation first.
> 3. **Always** state the operational feasibility in a separate sentence — losing this comment costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Unit error trap:** Writing "BEP = 3750" without specifying "kg" is a common 0.5-mark deduction.

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

> **"(a) Explain the types of licenses and registrations mandatory for a small food business in India. Discuss the role of FSSAI, Trade License, GST, and MSME/Udyam registration. (7 Marks)"**
>
> **"(b) What is innovation management? Explain with suitable food-industry examples the different types of innovation (product, process, packaging, business model). How does continuous innovation help in sustaining operations in a competitive environment? (7 Marks)"**

### ✅ Model Answer

#### Part (a): Mandatory Licenses and Registrations (7 Marks)

**1. FSSAI License/Registration (2 Marks):**
- **Issuing Authority:** Food Safety and Standards Authority of India
- **Three Tiers:** Basic (turnover < ₹12 lakh), State (₹12 lakh – ₹20 crore), Central (> ₹20 crore)
- **Role:** Ensures food safety, hygiene, and accurate labelling. Mandatory under FSS Act, 2006.
- **Document Required:** Form A/B submission, blueprint of premises, water analysis report, list of equipment, food safety management plan.

**2. Trade License (1.5 Marks):**
- **Issuing Authority:** Local Municipal Corporation/Municipality/Panchayat
- **Role:** Authorizes the use of premises for commercial food activity. Ensures compliance with building bylaws, fire safety, and zoning.
- **Validity:** Typically 1 year; renewable.

**3. GST Registration (1.5 Marks):**
- **Issuing Authority:** GSTN (Goods and Services Tax Network)
- **Threshold:** Mandatory if turnover > ₹40 lakh for goods (₹20 lakh for special category states)
- **Role:** Enables legal inter-state commerce, input tax credit, and e-commerce sales.

**4. MSME/Udyam Registration (1 Mark):**
- **Issuing Authority:** Ministry of MSME
- **Role:** Provides access to priority sector lending, lower interest rates, government subsidies, and easier compliance. Classification based on investment in plant & machinery and turnover.

**Other Registrations (1 Mark — Supportive Content):**
- Shop & Establishment Act (employee welfare)
- Factory License (manufacturing units under Factories Act)
- Pollution NOC (for effluent-generating units)
- Import-Export Code (DGFT) for exporters
- Trademark Registration (brand protection)

---

#### Part (b): Innovation Management & Sustained Operations (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Innovation Management is the systematic process of generating, evaluating, developing, and commercializing new ideas related to products, processes, packaging, or business models to create stakeholder value and competitive differentiation.

**Types of Innovation (4 Marks):**

| Type | Definition | Food-Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Innovation** | New or significantly improved food product | ITC's Aashirvaad Multi-grain Atta; plant-based meat by GoodDot |
| **Process Innovation** | New production/preservation technology | HPP (High-Pressure Processing) by Raw Pressery; freeze-drying by Decathlon |
| **Packaging Innovation** | Sustainable or smart packaging | Tetra Pak's aseptic packaging; edible spoons by Bakey's; time-temperature indicators in dairy |
| **Business Model Innovation** | Novel revenue/distribution model | Cloud kitchens by Rebel Foods; subscription meal kits by FreshMenu; D2C by The Whole Truth |

**Sustaining Operations Through Innovation (2 Marks):**
Continuous innovation ensures:
- **Defensive Innovation:** Prevents competitor disruption by staying ahead of trends.
- **Customer Retention:** Fresh offerings reduce churn; subscription models lock in loyalty.
- **Premium Pricing:** Unique innovations justify higher margins.
- **Operational Efficiency:** Process innovations reduce waste and cost (e.g., lean manufacturing cuts production cost by 15–20%).
- **Regulatory Future-Proofing:** Proactive innovation in clean-label, sustainable packaging pre-empts regulatory shifts.

**Real-World Case — Mamaearth (0.5 Mark):**
Mamaearth sustained operations by continuously innovating — from toxin-free baby products to natural ingredient adult care, expanding SKU portfolio while maintaining a "natural" brand promise. This sustained a CAC:LTV of < 1:5 and enabled profitability without heavy discounting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Theoretical:**
> 1. **Common Mistake:** Writing "Innovation = New Product" only. Examiners expect **4 types** with at least one example each (1 mark deducted per missing type).
> 2. **Terminology Trap:** "Sustaining operations" is NOT the same as "Growth" — students often conflate the two. Sustaining means *continuing to exist profitably*, not just expanding.
> 3. **FSSAI vs Trade License Confusion:** Examiners frequently include a sub-part testing this distinction. Mix-up costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Numerical Mistake:** In BEP problems, forgetting to subtract variable cost from selling price (using SP alone as contribution) is the most common error — full 0 mark for that part.

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the KTU ESE.**

### 🏁 Quick-Recall Bullet Points

- **Small Business** is defined under MSME based on **investment + turnover**; food businesses < ₹10 cr investment qualify as small.
- **Six Pillars of Management:** Strategic Planning, Financial, HR, Operations, Marketing, Compliance — all are interdependent.
- **FROMC Mnemonic for Start-up Challenges:** **F**inancial, **R**egulatory, **O**perational, **M**arketing, **C**ompetitive.
- **FSSAI Tiers:** Basic (< ₹12L), State (₹12L–₹20Cr), Central (> ₹20Cr). Validity: **5 years**.
- **FSSAI vs Trade License:** FSSAI = food safety; Trade License = location authorization. **Both are mandatory**.
- **Mandatory Licenses:** FSSAI + Trade + GST + Shop & Establishment + MSME (Udyam). Add Factory, Pollution NOC, IEC if applicable.
- **Break-Even Point:** BEP (units) = FC / (SP – VC). Always compute contribution first.
- **Contribution Margin Ratio** = Contribution / Sales × 100. Indicates operational efficiency.
- **ROI** = Net Profit / Investment × 100. > 15% is healthy for small food businesses.
- **Current Ratio** ideal = 1.5–2.0. **Quick Ratio** ideal = > 1.0.
- **CAC:LTV Ratio** — ideal is 1:3 or better. Below 1:3 indicates unsustainable unit economics.
- **CAGR** is the geometric mean growth rate, not arithmetic mean. Use the formula $(E/S)^{1/n} - 1$.
- **5 Types of Innovation:** Product, Process, Packaging, Business Model, Marketing.
- **4 Pillars of Sustained Competitive Advantage:** Differentiation, Cost Leadership, Niche Focus, Strategic Alliances.
- **Innovation Management Cycle:** Idea → Screen → Feasibility → Prototype → Pilot → Test Market → Launch → Review → Iterate.
- **Safety Stock** = Max Lead Time Demand − Average Lead Time Demand.
- **Reorder Level** = Max Consumption × Max Lead Time (effectively = Max Lead Time Demand).

- For **14-mark Part B questions**, always use **sub-headings, tables, and bullet points** — examiners value visual organization.
- For **numerical questions**, show **all working steps** — full marks require the journey, not just the answer.
- **Use food-industry examples** in every theoretical answer — generic management answers lose marks.
- **Memorize the formulas** in Section 2.6 with their **units and ideal values**.
